1: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος ἡ ἀληθινή — the seventh and final predicated I-AM saying of the Gospel. Ampelos (G288) — vine. Alēthinós (G228) — genuine, real, as opposed to merely alēthēs (accurate, truthful). The distinction matters: Israel was the vine (Ps 80:8; Isa 5:1–7; Jer 2:21), but a vine that produced sour grapes, withered and failed. Jesus is the alēthinē vine — the genuine article, the fulfillment of what Israel's vine was always meant to be, not merely a true-rather-than-false vine. ὁ πατήρ μου ὁ γεωργός ἐστιν — "my Father is the gardener." Geōrgós (G1092) — farmer, vine-dresser, one who works the earth. The Father is the active cultivator, tending the vine and its branches with purposeful skill.
2:
πᾶν κλῆμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μὴ φέρον καρπόν, αἴρει αὐτό — "every branch in me not bearing fruit, he takes away." Aírō (G142) — to lift up and take away. The same verb is used for taking away sin (1:29) and lifting the blind man's mat (5:8). Here it is removal from the vine.

καὶ πᾶν τὸ καρπὸν φέρον, καθαίρει αὐτό — "and every branch bearing fruit, he prunes it." Kathaírō (G2508) — to cleanse, to prune. The wordplay in Greek is audible: aírō (take away) and kathaírō (prune/cleanse) share the root airō. English loses this entirely. The action of the Father is not neutral: he removes the fruitless and purifies the fruitful. Both are active interventions in the vine.
3: ἤδη ὑμεῖς καθαροί ἐστε διὰ τὸν λόγον ὃν λελάληκα ὑμῖν — "already you are clean because of the word I have spoken to you." The verb kathairomai from v2 (prune/cleanse) generates its adjective katharoi here — clean. The disciples have already been pruned by the word. The perfect lelalēka — "I have spoken and it stands spoken" — grounds their cleanness in a completed speech-act of Jesus. This echoes 13:10 ("you are clean, but not every one of you") — a cleanness that is real but not universal among the Twelve.
4: μείνατε ἐν ἐμοί, κἀγὼ ἐν ὑμῖν — "remain in me, and I in you." The aorist imperative meínate — "begin and continue to remain" — issues the central command of the vine discourse. Menō (G3306) occurs 11 times in 15:1–16, making it the structural verb of the passage. καθὼς τὸ κλῆμα οὐ δύναται καρπὸν φέρειν ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ — "just as the branch cannot bear fruit of itself." Ou dúnatai — cannot, not merely does not. The incapacity is organic: separated from the vine, the branch has no sap, no life, no possibility of fruit. The analogy holds for the disciples: spiritual fruitfulness is not a human achievement but a product of union.
5: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος, ὑμεῖς τὰ κλήματα — the I-AM saying is repeated in its shorter form (without alēthinē), now paired with explicit identification of the disciples as branches. The juxtaposition is stark: "I am the vine; you are the branches." The grammar keeps the distinction: Jesus is the vine-stock itself, the source; the disciples are the extensions. ὁ μένων ἐν ἐμοὶ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ οὗτος φέρει καρπὸν πολύν — "the one remaining in me and I in him — this one bears much fruit." The mutual indwelling is inseparable from fruitfulness. χωρὶς ἐμοῦ οὐ δύνασθε ποιεῖν οὐδέν — "apart from me you can do nothing." Chōrís (G5565) — apart from, separate from. Ouden — nothing, absolutely nothing. No qualification; the totality of fruitful work depends on union with Christ.
6: ἐὰν μή τις μένῃ ἐν ἐμοί, ἐβλήθη ἔξω ὡς τὸ κλῆμα — "if anyone does not remain in me, he is thrown outside like the branch." The aorist passive eblēthē — "was thrown" — is gnomic aorist, expressing a typical or certain result as if it has already occurred. καὶ ἐξηράνθη — "and withered." The drying out follows inevitably from separation; it is not imposed but natural. καὶ συνάγουσιν αὐτὰ καὶ εἰς τὸ πῦρ βάλλουσιν — "and they gather them and throw them into the fire." The third-person plural "they" is indefinite — "people gather them" — focusing on the process, not an agent. The image of fire echoes the Baptist's warning (Matt 3:10; Luke 3:9) about trees without fruit being cut and burned.
7: ἐὰν μείνητε ἐν ἐμοὶ καὶ τὰ ῥήματά μου ἐν ὑμῖν μείνῃ — "if you remain in me and my words remain in you." Two conditions, both present subjunctive: the mutual remaining of disciples in Jesus and of Jesus's words (rhēmata) in the disciples. Rhēmata (plural of rhēma, G4487) — the specific sayings, the individual words — as opposed to logos (the unified word or message). ὃ ἐὰν θέλητε αἰτήσασθε — "ask whatever you wish." The aorist imperative aitēsasthe — the command to petition. The condition on asking is not arbitrary desire but the double remaining: one who remains in Jesus and has Jesus's words remaining in them will, by that very formation, want what the Father wills.
8: ἐν τούτῳ ἐδοξάσθη ὁ πατήρ μου, ἵνα καρπὸν πολὺν φέρητε — "in this is my Father glorified — that you bear much fruit." The aorist passive edoxasthē — "was glorified" — is again gnomic: the bearing of fruit constitutes the Father's glorification as a realized reality. καὶ γένησθε ἐμοὶ μαθηταί — "and you become my disciples." Génēsthe (G1096, aorist subjunctive of ginomai) — become, come to be. Discipleship is not a static status but an ongoing becoming that fruit-bearing both expresses and constitutes.
9: καθὼς ἠγάπησέν με ὁ πατήρ, κἀγὼ ὑμᾶς ἠγάπησα — "as the Father loved me, I also loved you." Both verbs are aorist — completed historical acts of love. The Father's love for the Son and the Son's love for the disciples are structurally parallel: the same kind of love, the same verb, the same tense. This is not analogy but derivation — Jesus's love for the disciples flows from and replicates the Father's love for him. μείνατε ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ τῇ ἐμῇ — "remain in my love." The aorist imperative meínate here modifies the key Johannine command: abiding in Jesus (vv4–7) is now specified as abiding in his love. Love is not an addition to union with Christ — it is what union with Christ feels like from the inside.
10: ἐὰν τὰς ἐντολάς μου τηρήσητε, μενεῖτε ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ μου — "if you keep my commandments, you will remain in my love." The future indicative meneite — this is not a conditional threat but a description of how remaining-in-love works: obedience is the relational channel. καθὼς ἐγὼ τὰς ἐντολὰς τοῦ πατρός μου τετήρηκα καὶ μένω ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ αὐτοῦ — "as I have kept my Father's commandments and remain in his love." Two tenses: perfect tetērēka ("I have kept and it stands kept") + present menō ("I remain"). Jesus's own Sonship is the template for the disciples' discipleship: his keeping of the Father's commands is both the model and the ground of theirs.
11: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα ἡ χαρὰ ἡ ἐμὴ ἐν ὑμῖν ᾖ — "these things I have spoken to you so that my joy may be in you." The perfect lelalēka again — completed disclosure. The purpose clause specifies the goal: not obedience for its own sake but the transmission of Christ's own joy. Chará (G5479, joy) — the noun from chaírō (to rejoice). καὶ ἡ χαρὰ ὑμῶν πληρωθῇ — "and your joy may be fulfilled/completed." Plēroō (G4137, aorist passive subjunctive) — to fill to fullness. The same verb used for Scripture's fulfillment; joy is "fulfilled" as a promise or vessel is filled to completion. Joy is the goal and product of abiding, not a byproduct.
12: αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ ἐντολὴ ἡ ἐμή, ἵνα ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους — "this is my commandment: that you love one another." The singular entolē — "commandment" — here, after the plural "commandments" in vv10, 21, 14:15. The plural commands are gathered into one: mutual love. καθὼς ἠγάπησα ὑμᾶς — "as I loved you." The aorist again — Jesus's completed act of love is the standard and measure. "Love one another" without this qualifier would be ambiguous; "as I loved you" sets the definition by what will happen at the cross.
13: μείζονα ταύτης ἀγάπην οὐδεὶς ἔχει — "greater than this love no one has." The superlative of agapē is defined by its limit: giving life. Meizōna (G3187) — greater, highest — comparative used for the superlative. ἵνα τις τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ θῇ ὑπὲρ τῶν φίλων αὐτοῦ — "that one lays down his life for his friends." Psuchēn (G5590) — soul, life, the whole self. Títhēmi (G5087) — to lay down, to put — the verb Jesus consistently uses for his own death (10:11, 15, 17, 18). The present subjunctive thē presents the action as a particular case. The "friends" (philoi) here are the disciples — not the universal humanitarian but the covenanted community. This verse defines the standard that v12 ("love as I loved you") enacts.
14: ὑμεῖς φίλοι μού ἐστε ἐὰν ποιῆτε ἃ ἐγὼ ἐντέλλομαι ὑμῖν — "you are my friends if you do what I command you." The conditional is present subjunctive — ongoing doing. Phílos (G5384) — friend, one who is dear, a beloved companion. In the patronage world, philos carried significant social weight: a friend of the patron was one who had privileged access and trust. The condition — doing what Jesus commands — is not a transactional requirement but an organic description: friendship with Jesus is constituted by alignment with his will, as obedience to the Father constitutes the Son's relationship with him.
15: οὐκέτι λέγω ὑμᾶς δούλους — "no longer do I call you servants/slaves." Doúlos (G1401) — slave, bond-servant. In the honor-shame world, the slave does not know the master's plans — only instructions. ὑμᾶς δὲ εἴρηκα φίλους — "but I have called you friends." Perfect eirēka — a declaration that stands. ὅτι πάντα ἃ ἤκουσα παρὰ τοῦ πατρός μου ἐγνώρισα ὑμῖν — "because all that I heard from my Father I have made known to you." Two aorists: ēkousa (I heard) and egnōrisa (I made known). The disclosure is complete and total — panta (all things). The disciples are philoi because they have been admitted into the counsel of God that Jesus received from the Father.
16: οὐχ ὑμεῖς με ἐξελέξασθε, ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ ἐξελεξάμην ὑμᾶς — "not you chose me, but I chose you." Both verbs are aorist: the election is a completed historical act. Eklégomai (G1586) — to choose, to select out from a larger group. The emphatic pronoun contrast — ouch hymeis vs. all᾽ egō — makes the priority of the divine initiative absolute. καὶ ἔθηκα ὑμᾶς ἵνα ὑμεῖς ὑπάγητε καὶ καρπὸν φέρητε — "and I appointed you so that you might go and bear fruit." Títhēmi (G5087) — to place, to appoint — used for laying down the life (10:11), now for commissioning. The election is purposive: chosen for mission, not merely for status. καρπὸς ὑμῶν μένῃ — "your fruit might remain." Menō again: the fruit produced by those remaining in the vine is itself characterized by remaining — enduring, permanent results.
17: ταῦτα ἐντέλλομαι ὑμῖν, ἵνα ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους — the command of v12 is repeated exactly: the vine discourse returns to its ethical center. The repetition brackets the intermediate material (vv13–16) as the ground and explication of mutual love. The Evangelist frames the commandment twice: it is the command, and it requires a reason, and the reason is given between the two statements of the command.
18: εἰ ὁ κόσμος ὑμᾶς μισεῖ, γινώσκετε ὅτι ἐμὲ πρῶτον ὑμῶν μεμίσηκεν — "if the world hates you, know that it has hated me before you." The perfect memísēken — "has hated and continues to hate." Jesus's being-hated is a completed, standing fact that precedes and explains the disciples' experience. Ginōskete can be indicative ("you know") or imperative ("know!") — either reading makes sense: the disciples either already know this or need to know it as a ground for resilience.
19: εἰ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου ἦτε, ὁ κόσμος ἂν τὸ ἴδιον ἐφίλει — "if you were of the world, the world would love its own." The conditional is past contrary-to-fact: ēte (imperfect, you were) + an ephílei (would love). To ídion — "its own" — the world loves what belongs to it, what shares its nature. ὅτι δὲ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ ἐστέ, ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ ἐξελεξάμην ὑμᾶς ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου — "but because you are not of the world, but I chose you out of the world." The election in v16 now has a negative implication: to be chosen out of the world is to be set against it. Ek tou kosmou — "out of the world" — spatial metaphor of extraction from a domain.
20: μνημονεύετε τοῦ λόγου οὗ ἐγὼ εἶπον ὑμῖν — "remember the word that I said to you." Mnēmoneuete (G3421, present imperative) — keep on remembering. The word is the servant-master proverb: "a servant is not greater than his master." This appeared in the footwashing scene (13:16); here it is applied to persecution: the treatment of the master predicts and defines the treatment of the servants. εἰ ἐμὲ ἐδίωξαν, καὶ ὑμᾶς διώξουσιν — "if they persecuted me, they will also persecute you." The aorist ediōxan and the future diōxousin frame the disciples' future in the pattern of Jesus's past.
21: ἀλλὰ ταῦτα πάντα ποιήσουσιν ὑμῖν διὰ τὸ ὄνομά μου — "but all these things they will do to you on account of my name." The phrase dia to onoma mou — "on account of my name" — makes the persecution christological: the disciples are targeted not for their own sake but because of their association with Jesus. ὅτι οὐκ οἴδασιν τὸν πέμψαντά με — "because they do not know the one who sent me." The perfect oidasin — "they have not come to know and it stands unknown." The world's hatred of Jesus and his followers stems from its fundamental not-knowing of the Father.
22: εἰ μὴ ἦλθον καὶ ἐλάλησα αὐτοῖς, ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ εἴχοσαν — "if I had not come and spoken to them, they would not have sin." The condition is past contrary-to-fact: ēlthon and elalēsa (aorists of real events) are cast counterfactually. The point is not that Jesus's coming creates sin but that it removes the excuse for not-knowing. νῦν δὲ πρόφασιν οὐκ ἔχουσιν περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας αὐτῶν — "but now they have no excuse for their sin." Próphasis (G4392) — a pretext, a plausible reason for denial. The coming of the light makes darkness inexcusable.
23: ὁ ἐμὲ μισῶν καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου μισεῖ — "the one hating me also hates my Father." The logic of Johannine identity: to hate the Son is to hate the one who sent him, because to see the Son is to see the Father (14:9). There is no access to the Father that bypasses the Son. The two loves and two hates are structurally identical.
24: εἰ τὰ ἔργα μὴ ἐποίησα ἐν αὐτοῖς ἃ οὐδεὶς ἄλλος ἐποίησεν — "if I had not done among them the works that no one else did." Oudeis allos — no one else. The works of Jesus are categorically unique — not merely impressive but without precedent. ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ εἴχοσαν — "they would not have sin" — the same construction as v22: the works, like the words, remove the excuse of ignorance. Rejection after seeing the unique works is culpable rejection.
25: ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα πληρωθῇ ὁ λόγος ὁ ἐν τῷ νόμῳ αὐτῶν γεγραμμένος — "but that the word written in their law might be fulfilled." Plēroō (G4137, aorist passive subjunctive) — the fulfillment formula. The phrase "their law" is not dismissive but rhetorically pointed: Jesus cites their own scriptures against them. ὅτι ἐμίσησάν με δωρεάν — "that they hated me without cause." Dōreán (G1432) — gratis, freely, without payment or reason. The citation draws from Ps 35:19 or 69:4. The hatred is gratuitous: there is no provocation in Jesus that grounds it; the antipathy is self-originating in the world's alienation from God.
26: ὅταν ἔλθῃ ὁ παράκλητος ὃν ἐγὼ πέμψω ὑμῖν παρὰ τοῦ πατρός — the third Paraclete saying, and here the sending agent shifts again to Jesus: "whom I will send to you from the Father." In 14:16, the Father sends in response to the Son's prayer; in 14:26, the Father sends in the Son's name; here, the Son sends from the Father. The two missions (Father-sends, Son-sends) are held in tension without contradiction, reflecting the coinherence of the persons. τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας ὃ παρὰ τοῦ πατρὸς ἐκπορεύεται — "the Spirit of truth who proceeds from the Father." Ekporeúomai (G1607) — to go out from, to proceed out of. This verb became the focus of the Filioque controversy (whether the Spirit proceeds from the Father alone or from the Father and the Son). The verse says "from the Father" without "and the Son" — the sending (economic mission) and the eternal procession (ontological origin) are distinct questions. ἐκεῖνος μαρτυρήσει περὶ ἐμοῦ — "that one will testify concerning me." The Spirit's primary role here is witness to Jesus, not independent revelation.
27: καὶ ὑμεῖς δὲ μαρτυρεῖτε — "and you also testify." Present imperative — ongoing witness. The disciples bear witness alongside the Spirit: two witnesses, consistent with the requirement of Deut 17:6 and 19:15. The ground for their witness: ὅτι ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἐστε — "because you have been with me from the beginning." Perfect participle implied: their continuous presence from the beginning qualifies them as eyewitnesses. "The beginning" echoes 1:1 and 1 John 1:1 — the Johannine community's foundational claim to original witness.
